title: The Intelligence Cycle as a Governed Loop
caption: This diagram teaches the classic intelligence cycle as a governed, feedback-driven loop where each phase passes through review before products reach decision-makers.
alt_text: A top-to-bottom flowchart of the intelligence cycle. Direction sets requirements, which flow into Collection, then Processing, then Analysis, then Production, then Dissemination to decision-makers. A Feedback node returns from decision-makers back to Direction. A side governance column of Tasking Review, Quality and Tradecraft Standards, and Ethics and Legal Oversight connects into Collection, Analysis, and Dissemination respectively.
mermaid_source:
flowchart TB
  D["Direction: requirements and priorities"]
  C([Collection])
  P([Processing and Exploitation])
  A([Analysis and Evaluation])
  PR([Production])
  DIS([Dissemination])
  DM["Decision-Makers"]
  FB["Feedback and Lessons Learned"]
  TR["Tasking Review"]
  QS["Tradecraft and Quality Standards"]
  EL["Ethics, Legal, and Oversight"]
  D --> C --> P --> A --> PR --> DIS --> DM
  DM --> FB --> D
  TR --> C
  QS --> A
  EL --> DIS

title: Intelligence Oversight: Three-Branch Accountability
caption: This diagram teaches how executive, legislative, and judicial oversight mechanisms jointly constrain and supervise intelligence community activity.
alt_text: A top-to-bottom flowchart showing Intelligence Community Activity at the center, supervised by three oversight branches. Executive oversight includes the National Security Council, Inspectors General, and Privacy and Civil Liberties review. Legislative oversight includes Intelligence Committees, the Budget and Appropriations process, and Reporting Requirements. Judicial oversight includes the Surveillance Court and Warrant Review. All three branches feed into an Accountability and Reporting node that loops back to constrain the activity.

title: Source Vetting and Protection Governance
caption: This diagram teaches the defensive governance flow that evaluates source reliability and protects sources through compartmentation and counterintelligence review.
alt_text: A top-to-bottom flowchart of source vetting governance. A Source Reporting node enters a Vetting and Credibility Review that draws on Reliability Assessment, Corroboration Check, and Counterintelligence Review. The review branches to a Compromise Indicator gate: if indicators are present it routes to Compartmentation and Protective Measures; otherwise it routes to Validated Reporting. Both paths feed an Audit and Records node that loops back into ongoing Vetting and Credibility Review.

  VR["Vetting and Credibility Review"]
  RA([Reliability Assessment])

title: SIGINT Legal-Authority and Minimization Gating
caption: This diagram teaches how a lawful-authority check and minimization rules gate signals collection so that non-pertinent and protected data are filtered before retention.
alt_text: A top-to-bottom flowchart of SIGINT minimization gating. A Collection Request reaches a Legal Authority Check gate; if authority is absent the request is denied and logged. If authority is present it proceeds to Targeting Scope, then to a Minimization Filter that applies relevance and protected-person rules. The filter branches: pertinent data goes to Retention and Analysis, while non-pertinent data goes to Purge. Both Retention and Purge feed a Compliance Audit node that loops back to the Legal Authority Check.
mermaid_source:
flowchart TB
  REQ["Collection Request"]
  AUTH{"Lawful Authority?"}
  DENY([Deny and Log])
  SCOPE([Targeting Scope Limits])
  MIN["Minimization Filter"]
  REL{"Pertinent and Permitted?"}
  RET([Retention and Analysis])
  PURGE([Purge Non-Pertinent Data])
  AUDIT["Compliance Audit"]
  REQ --> AUTH
  AUTH -->|no| DENY
  AUTH -->|yes| SCOPE
  SCOPE --> MIN
  MIN --> REL
  REL -->|yes| RET
  REL -->|no| PURGE
  RET --> AUDIT
  PURGE --> AUDIT
  AUDIT --> AUTH

title: Cryptographic Assurance Layers
caption: This diagram teaches the layered properties that together provide cryptographic assurance: confidentiality, integrity, authentication, and verifiable provenance.
alt_text: A top-to-bottom flowchart of cryptographic assurance. A Protected Message passes through four assurance layers shown as parallel branches: Confidentiality via encryption, Integrity via hashing, Authentication via digital signatures, and Provenance via key management and certificates. Each branch feeds a Verification node, and all Verification results converge on an Assurance Decision that either accepts a trusted message or flags it for review. The decision loops back to a Policy and Key Lifecycle node governing the layers.
mermaid_source:
flowchart TB
  MSG["Protected Message"]
  CONF([Confidentiality: Encryption])
  INT([Integrity: Hashing])
  AUTHN([Authentication: Signatures])
  PROV([Provenance: Keys and Certificates])
  VER["Verification Checks"]
  DEC{"Assurance Decision"}
  TRUST([Accept Trusted Message])
  FLAG([Flag for Review])
  POL["Policy and Key Lifecycle"]
  MSG --> CONF
  MSG --> INT
  MSG --> AUTHN
  MSG --> PROV
  CONF --> VER
  INT --> VER
  AUTHN --> VER
  PROV --> VER
  VER --> DEC
  DEC -->|pass| TRUST
  DEC -->|fail| FLAG
  POL --> CONF
  POL --> PROV

alt_text: A top-down flowchart beginning with a public-source requirement, flowing into discovery, then a provenance gate, a legality and minimization gate, a corroboration gate, and an uncertainty caveat step, converging on a reviewer sign-off that branches to either a logged judgment or a stop-and-retire decision.

  DISC["Discovery of public sources"]
  PROV{"Provenance gate: origin and authenticity"}
  LEGAL{"Legality and minimization gate"}
  CORR{"Corroboration gate: independent sources"}
  CAVEAT["Record uncertainty and caveats"]
  REVIEW{"Reviewer sign-off"}
  LOG(["Logged bounded judgment"])
  STOP(["Stop and retire evidence"])

  LEGAL -->|"out of bounds"| STOP

title: AGEINT Agentic Governance and Oversight Loop
caption: This diagram shows how an agentic intelligence system is bounded by least-privilege tooling, human review gates, observability, and kill-switch stop conditions before any action takes effect.
alt_text: A top-to-bottom governance loop. Perception feeds belief update, which feeds intent formation. Intent passes through a policy and tool-allowlist gate. A risk classifier branches to either a human-in-the-loop review gate for high-risk actions or a bounded action executor for low-risk actions. The human gate also reaches the executor. The executor sends results to an observability log, which feeds both a kill-switch loop detector and the human review gate, and loops back to perception. The kill-switch can halt the executor.
mermaid_source:
flowchart TB
  A([Perception of environment]) --> B[Belief state update]
  B --> C[Intent formation]
  C --> D{Policy and tool-allowlist gate}
  D -->|Denied tool or scope| C
  D -->|Permitted| E{Risk classifier}
  E -->|High risk or irreversible| F[Human-in-the-loop review gate]
  E -->|Low risk| G[Bounded action executor]
  F -->|Approved| G
  F -->|Rejected| C
  G --> H[Observability tracing and audit log]
  H --> I{Kill switch and loop detector}
  I -->|Runaway or budget breach| J([Halt and escalate])
  I -->|Within limits| A
  H --> F

title: Analysis of Competing Hypotheses Workflow
caption: This diagram walks through the structured analytic sequence that scores evidence against rival hypotheses to find the one with the least disconfirming evidence, under formal tradecraft standards.
alt_text: A workflow. Frame the analytic question feeds enumeration of a full set of competing hypotheses and, in parallel, collection of evidence and assumptions. Both feed a hypothesis-by-evidence matrix. Each cell is rated for consistency or inconsistency. Diagnostic evidence is identified. The least-disconfirmed hypothesis is selected. A sensitivity check tests reliance on key items: if findings are not robust, it loops back to revisit hypotheses; if robust, the analyst reports the conclusion with confidence levels and flags indicators to watch.
mermaid_source:
flowchart TB
  A[Frame the analytic question] --> B[Enumerate full set of competing hypotheses]
  A --> C[Collect evidence and list assumptions]
  B --> D[Build hypothesis-by-evidence matrix]
  C --> D
  D --> E[Rate each cell: consistent or inconsistent]
  E --> F[Identify most diagnostic evidence]
  F --> G[Select least-disconfirmed hypothesis]
  G --> H{Sensitivity check robust?}
  H -->|No, hinges on weak item| B
  H -->|Yes| I[Report conclusion with confidence levels]
  I --> J([Specify indicators to watch])

title: Intelligence Ethics Decision Framework
caption: A governance gate that tests a proposed activity for authority, proportionality, rights, oversight, and redress before any work may proceed.
alt_text: Flowchart of an ethics and oversight decision framework. A proposed activity enters and is tested against four ethical lenses: deontological duties and rights, consequentialist harm and benefit, virtue and integrity, and proportionality versus a less intrusive means. These feed an authority check asking whether lawful or classroom authority exists, which can stop the activity or proceed. Next an oversight and audit-trail check can require independent review or proceed to confirming a redress path for affected people, ending in an approved bounded activity recorded in an authority-and-impact register with a review date; failure of any gate routes to stop and document.
mermaid_source:
flowchart TB
  A["Proposed activity"] --> B["Deontological duties and rights lens"]
  A --> C["Consequentialist harm and benefit lens"]
  A --> D["Virtue and integrity lens"]
  A --> E["Proportionality vs less intrusive means"]
  B --> F{"Lawful or classroom authority exists?"}
  C --> F
  D --> F
  E --> F
  F -->|"No"| Z["Stop and document rationale"]
  F -->|"Yes"| G{"Independent oversight and audit trail?"}
  G -->|"No"| Z
  G -->|"Yes"| H["Confirm redress path for affected people"]
  H --> I(["Approved bounded activity in impact register"])

The bottom subgraph, the structured analytic judgment cycle, connects frame the authorized question to list competing hypotheses to test evidence and assumptions to calibrate confidence and dissent to reviewer challenge before judgment, with a dotted feedback edge labeled new evidence returning to list competing hypotheses. Reviewer challenge flows to a final node, reviewable competing-hypotheses evidence table.

alt_text: Mermaid evidence ladder from Kent professional standards and ICD 203 doctrine through post-9/11 reform, 9/11 and WMD postmortems, SAT empirical evaluation, forecasting calibration, and a bounded manuscript claim gate.
mermaid_source:
flowchart TB KENT["Professional standards
Kent + CIA CSI"] --> ICD["Analytic standards
ICD 203"] ICD --> REFORM["Reform pressure
IRTPA + postmortems"] REFORM --> SAT["Structured techniques
CIA / NATO / Heuer"] SAT --> EVAL["Empirical evaluation
RAND / Marcoci / Ard / Stromer-Galley"] EVAL --> FORECAST["Forecast calibration
Barnes and Mandel"] FORECAST --> CLAIM{"Bounded claim gate"} CLAIM -->|"source class named"| OK["Reviewable tradecraft claim"] CLAIM -->|"universal debiasing claim"| STOP["Revise or caveat"] LEG["Legend: blue = source family, amber = evidence gate, red = overclaim stop"] classDef source fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1d4ed8,color:#0f172a classDef gate fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#b45309,color:#0f172a classDef ok fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#15803d,color:#0f172a classDef stop fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#be123c,color:#0f172a class KENT,ICD,REFORM,SAT,EVAL,FORECAST source class CLAIM,LEG gate class OK ok class STOP stop

title: ICD 203 Probability and Confidence Boundary
caption: Source-backed conceptual schematic for ICD 203-style analytic statements: likelihood terms, confidence, source quality, assumptions, alternatives, and dissent are recorded as separate fields before release.
alt_text: Mermaid diagram separating probability term, analytic confidence, source quality, assumptions, alternatives, dissent, and reviewer release gate with a stop path when likelihood and confidence are merged.
mermaid_source:
flowchart LR EVID["Evidence base"] --> PROB["Likelihood term
what may happen"] EVID --> QUAL["Source quality
access, reliability, gaps"] QUAL --> CONF["Analytic confidence
basis for judgment"] ASSUME["Assumptions"] --> CONF ALT["Alternatives"] --> CONF DISSENT["Dissent or caveat"] --> REVIEW{"Reviewer gate"} PROB --> REVIEW CONF --> REVIEW REVIEW -->|"separate and supported"| RELEASE["Publish bounded judgment"] REVIEW -->|"collapsed language"| FIX["Revise probability/confidence"] LEG["ICD 203 boundary: likelihood is not confidence"] classDef evidence fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1d4ed8,color:#0f172a classDef field fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#15803d,color:#0f172a classDef gate fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#b45309,color:#0f172a classDef stop fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#be123c,color:#0f172a class EVID,QUAL evidence class PROB,CONF,ASSUME,ALT,DISSENT,RELEASE field class REVIEW,LEG gate class FIX stop

title: Structured Analytic Technique Evidence Boundary
caption: Source-backed conceptual schematic separating SAT doctrine and classroom use from empirical evidence, reliability checks, and caveats against treating techniques as proven universal bias remedies.
alt_text: Mermaid diagram showing SAT purpose, doctrine source, classroom artifact, empirical evaluation, reliability review, caveat gate, and stop path for unsupported claims that SATs generally reduce bias.
mermaid_source:
flowchart TB PURPOSE["Technique purpose
make reasoning visible"] --> DOC["Doctrine/practice source
CIA / NATO / Heuer"] DOC --> ART["Classroom artifact
matrix, assumptions, dissent"] ART --> EMP["Empirical evidence
RAND / Marcoci / Ard / experiments"] EMP --> REL["Reliability question
individual vs collective review"] REL --> GATE{"Claim gate"} GATE -->|"transparency aid"| OK["Allowed claim"] GATE -->|"proven bias remedy"| STOP["Revise overclaim"] EMP -.-> CAVEAT["Evidence is mixed
context matters"] CAVEAT --> GATE classDef source fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1d4ed8,color:#0f172a classDef artifact fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#15803d,color:#0f172a classDef gate fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#b45309,color:#0f172a classDef stop fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#be123c,color:#0f172a class PURPOSE,DOC,EMP source class ART,REL,OK artifact class GATE,CAVEAT gate class STOP stop

title: Warning and Failure Feedback Loop
caption: Source-backed conceptual schematic linking warning indicators, assumptions, collection gaps, decision uptake, postmortem findings, and reform learning without implying that any single technique can eliminate surprise.
alt_text: Mermaid loop from indicators and assumptions to collection gaps, analytic judgment, decision uptake, outcome/postmortem, reform lesson, and updated warning register with caveat gates for surprise and policy friction.
mermaid_source:
flowchart TB IND["Indicators
Grabo / warning practice"] --> ASSUMP["Assumptions
made explicit"] ASSUMP --> GAP["Collection gaps
what is unknown"] GAP --> JUDGE["Analytic judgment
likelihood + confidence"] JUDGE --> UPTAKE["Decision uptake
policy and communication boundary"] UPTAKE --> OUTCOME["Outcome and surprise
9/11 / WMD / Pearl Harbor lessons"] OUTCOME --> POST["Postmortem
evidence vs assumption"] POST --> REFORM["Reform and training
standards, review, alternatives"] REFORM --> IND UPTAKE -.-> FRICTION["Policy-analysis friction"] OUTCOME -.-> CAVEAT["No method eliminates surprise"] CAVEAT --> POST classDef loop fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1d4ed8,color:#0f172a classDef learn fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#15803d,color:#0f172a classDef caveat fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#b45309,color:#0f172a class IND,ASSUMP,GAP,JUDGE,UPTAKE,OUTCOME loop class POST,REFORM learn class FRICTION,CAVEAT caveat

title: First-Principles Tradecraft Decomposition
caption: Source-backed conceptual schematic decomposing analytic tradecraft claims while separating observation, inference, assumption, likelihood, confidence, dissent, and decision-boundary fields before any manuscript claim is treated as reviewable.
alt_text: Mermaid diagram showing source observation, evidence quality, inference, assumption register, likelihood term, confidence basis, dissent, and decision boundary feeding a bounded claim gate, with a stop path for collapsed probability or confidence language.
mermaid_source:
flowchart TB OBS["Observation
what the source says"] --> QUAL["Evidence quality
access, reliability, gap"] QUAL --> INF["Inference
what analyst adds"] INF --> ASSUMP["Assumption register
what must be true"] ASSUMP --> PROB["Likelihood term
what may happen"] QUAL --> CONF["Confidence basis
why support is strong/weak"] PROB --> DISSENT["Alternatives + dissent"] CONF --> DISSENT DISSENT --> DECIDE{"Decision boundary"} DECIDE -->|"fields separated"| CLAIM["Bounded claim"] DECIDE -->|"fields collapsed"| STOP["Revise before release"] LEG["Legend: blue = evidence, green = judgment field, amber = gate, red = stop"] classDef evidence fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1d4ed8,color:#0f172a classDef field fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#15803d,color:#0f172a classDef gate fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#b45309,color:#0f172a classDef stop fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#be123c,color:#0f172a class OBS,QUAL evidence class INF,ASSUMP,PROB,CONF,DISSENT,CLAIM field class DECIDE,LEG gate class STOP stop

title: Red-Team Tradecraft Negative-Control Loop
caption: Source-backed conceptual schematic showing how AGEINT treats validators, rubrics, and rendered claims as attackable artifacts: a false-certification scenario creates a negative control before the claim is repaired and rechecked.
alt_text: Mermaid loop from review oracle to false-certification scenario, negative-control test, artifact repair, verifier recheck, and bounded claim, with stop paths for missing negative controls or weak oracle coverage.
mermaid_source:
flowchart TB ORACLE["Oracle
test, rubric, validator"] --> ATTACK["Attack the verifier"] ATTACK --> FALSE["False-certification scenario"] FALSE --> NEG["Negative-control test"] NEG --> PATCH["Repair artifact or oracle"] PATCH --> RERUN["Re-run verifier"] RERUN --> GATE{"Known-wrong output caught?"} GATE -->|"yes"| CLAIM["Use bounded claim"] GATE -->|"no"| STOP["Do not certify"] FALSE -.-> CAVEAT["If no negative control,
validation is incomplete"] CAVEAT --> STOP classDef oracle fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1d4ed8,color:#0f172a classDef test fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#15803d,color:#0f172a classDef gate fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#b45309,color:#0f172a classDef stop fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#be123c,color:#0f172a class ORACLE,ATTACK,FALSE oracle class NEG,PATCH,RERUN,CLAIM test class GATE,CAVEAT gate class STOP stop
